does: This paper investigates whether large language models used as judges for task evaluation actually follow their evaluation instructions or instead rely on inherent preferences learned during training. The authors systematically vary the specificity and content of evaluation prompts across multiple state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4, Llama3) and compare LLM judgments against human evaluations. They construct a taxonomy of quality criteria from existing evaluations and test how much explicit prompting influences the alignment between AI judgments and human preferences. The paper also introduces a prompt-free baseline using model perplexity as an alternative quality measure, enabling direct measurement of how much instruction adherence versus learned biases drive LLM-as-a-judge performance.
connects: This work addresses a critical assumption underlying the recent explosion of LLM-based evaluation methods popularized by systems like MT-Bench and Arena. It responds to growing concerns about whether RLHF-aligned models truly follow task-specific instructions or instead exhibit preference biases from their training data. The paper builds on prior work in prompt engineering and instruction-following benchmarks while extending these concepts to the evaluation domain. It connects to broader research on AI alignment and whether fine-tuned models genuinely internalize instructions versus displaying learned patterns. The comparison against perplexity-based evaluation relates to earlier work on reference-free evaluation metrics and the distinction between semantic understanding and statistical pattern matching in language models.
enables: These findings suggest several future directions for improving automated evaluation systems. Understanding the gap between instruction adherence and learned preferences could enable development of more reliable LLM-as-a-judge systems through better prompt engineering, constraint-based decoding, or architectural modifications. The work points toward research on evaluating evaluator trustworthiness and establishing when LLM judgments can safely replace human evaluation versus when human-in-the-loop assessment remains necessary. It also opens questions about developing evaluation benchmarks specifically designed to test instruction-following robustness, and could motivate work on training methods that improve adherence to explicit evaluation criteria over implicit training biases.
